seoTitle: How to Apply a Supplier Credit in QuickBooks
metaDescription: Learn how to record a vendor credit in QuickBooks and apply it against an open bill to reduce what your business owes.
intro: Getting a credit note from a supplier for returned goods or an overpayment needs to be tracked properly so it actually offsets what you owe. This guide covers entering a Vendor Credit in QuickBooks, mapping it to the right expense category, and applying it against an open bill through the Pay Bills workspace. It's for bookkeepers managing accounts payable adjustments.

steps.heading: Open the Vendor Credit form
steps.body: From the global create menu, select Vendor Credit to start recording the supplier's adjustment.

steps.heading: Choose the vendor and map the category
steps.body: Select the supplier profile, then choose the expense account that matches the original purchase being credited.

steps.heading: Enter the credit amount
steps.body: Type in the exact dollar amount of the credit as provided by the supplier's credit note.

steps.heading: Save the credit record
steps.body: Save the vendor credit to update your accounts payable balance and reduce your total liabilities to that supplier.

steps.heading: Apply the credit to an open bill
steps.body: Open the Pay Bills workspace, select the open bill, and match it against the available vendor credit balance to reduce the amount due.

faq.question: Does a vendor credit reduce my accounts payable right away?
faq.answer: Yes, saving the vendor credit immediately reduces your total outstanding liability to that supplier.

faq.question: Can I apply a credit to a bill from a different vendor?
faq.answer: No, vendor credits can only be applied to bills from the same supplier that issued the credit.

faq.question: What if the credit is larger than any open bill?
faq.answer: The remaining credit balance will stay available on the vendor's account until you have a future bill to apply it against.

faq.question: How can I check my supplier's current credit balance?
faq.answer: Run a Vendor Balance Summary report to see the current standing and any unapplied credits for each supplier.
